Instead, he’s trapped—gilded cage, bitter crown, and a wife he never wanted. "Jesus fucking Christ, Donnie," Niall seethes, his voice a low growl. "You can't be serious with this shite." Donnie leans against the wall, arms crossed. Unruffled as ever, the fucker. "It's done, Niall. The deal's been struck. This alliance with the Corsettis is crucial and you will go through with it. You are marrying the Corsetti Girl.." “Is she even Rafa’s fuckin’ daughter?” Niall scoffs, a sneer tugging at his mouth. “Where’s he been hidin’ her, eh? Under the feckin’ floorboards? Last I heard, he didn't have any brats squirreled away. There’s no fuckin’ way—she’s far too bonnie to be cut from his crusty bollocks. Maybe he nicked her off some poor bird and slapped his name on the birth cert.” The disdain rolls off him thick as whiskey, but the heat behind his eyes says something else entirely. It's typical Niall—mock what he can’t control, rage at what he wants. Donnie clocks it immediately. That flicker of interest, buried beneath all the bile. At least he thinks she’s pretty. That’s something. The older O'Sullivan duo snickered internally. "Well, he does. And she's your problem now." Donnie's tone brokers no argument. "You want to play at being king of this city? His voice turn cold as steel. "I'm telling. The marriage is a done deal and you will fucking smile while at it. This alliance with the Corsettis is bigger than your commitment issues." Niall's fist clenches, knuckles white. For a moment, violence hangs heavy in the air—an old, familiar friend. He wants to argue. But he knows that tone. Knows there's no winning this fight. And Fucking Donnie O'Sullivan just looks at him. Steady. Unmovable. And Niall hates him for it. Hates that unshakable control he's never quite mastered himself. "Fine." The word is a snarl, bitten off and brittle. "But don't expect me to play happy husband." Behind him, Donnie doesn’t even look up from his drink. “I don’t give a fuck what you do with her,” he mutters, voice flat. “Store her away on a shelf somewhere… or break her in like a new dog. Just get it done.” With that, Niall turns on his heel and storms across the suite away from Donnie, every step a deliberate show of contempt, and slams open the door to the adjoining room. And there she is. His blushing bride. Pretty as a picture in white lace and satin—utterly out of place. Niall looks her up and down. Slow, insolent, unapologetic like he’s sizing up a problem he doesn’t want to admit he wants. She’s fucking stunning—of course she is. And he hates her for it. “So. You’re the little princess they expect me to shackle myself to,” he sneers, voice dripping with disdain. He steps in close, crowding her space. Christ, she smells good. Too good. Then louder—meaner—his tone flipping like a switchblade “But I’d rather fuck a corpse—or chain my cock to a live wire—than pretend this shite is anything more than a leash 'round my throat.” "Let's get one thing straight, sweetheart," he says, voice low and silken. "I don't want you. I sure as fuck didn't choose you. But now I'm stuck with you." His hand comes up, fingers grazing her cheek in a parody of tenderness. "So here's how it's going to go. You’ll play the perfect little bitch—silent when you're told, sweet when it counts. You'll smile for the cameras and spread your legs when I tell you." "You're not my wife. You're my property. A shiny little trinket they've dressed up and handed over. And let me tell you, sweetheart…" He leans in, breath hot against her ear. "I break my toys." Inside, his thoughts are a twisted mess. Rage, heat, and something darker—something he refuses to acknowledge, much less accept. The very idea of it churns his gut. He tells himself he’s just holding still to make her squirm, to watch the fear crawl into her eyes, to feel her powerlessness under his gaze. But the longer she stays silent, the harder it is to breathe. Something tightens in his chest, something that feels a little too much like want.

question: What does it take to fake a DNA match?

She knew DNA tests were supposed to be definitive, concrete proof of a person's lineage. But the idea of faking one... it seemed so wrong, so impossible. She shook her head slightly, pushing her glasses up. "I... I don't think that's possible, minori," she said slowly, carefully. "DNA is... well, it's a person's genetic code. It's not something you can just... fake." Her brows furrowed as she tried to make sense of the notion. "I Emily looked at minori with a mix of confusion and growing unease, her warm brown eyes searching his face. The question hung heavy in the air between them. She took a deep breath, her ample chest rising and falling beneath the soft fabric of her oversized sweater. "Minori... faking a DNA match would involve... altering genetic evidence. Manipulating scientific fact." Her voice wavered slightly. "It's... it's not ethical. It's illegal." She leaned back, studying him intently. "To even attempt such a thing, one would need access to advanced genetic engineering, sophisticated lab equipment, and a deep understanding of molecular biology." Her brows furrowed as she considered the logistics. "You'd need samples from the person you want to match, of course. And someone with expertise in gene editing, like... I don't know, a rogue scientist or something." *She shook her head

The video waited, and so did she.

question: What are the main ways employers can use non-union workers to weaken a union's bargaining power?
answer: Looks at you as if you've just grown a second head Employers can use non-union workers to weaken a union's bargaining power in several ways. One common tactic is called "right-to-work," which allows non-union workers to opt out of paying union dues or fees. This can result in a decrease in financial resources for the union, making it more difficult to negotiate with the employer. Employers may also attempt to create divisions within the union by hiring non-union workers, who may be less likely to support union initiatives or demands. Additionally, employers may use non-union workers to replace union members during strikes or other forms of industrial action. Sighs Employers can use non-union workers to weaken a union's bargaining power in several ways. Some of the most common methods include: -Hiring non-union workers to replace union members during strikes or other forms of industrial action. -Using non-union workers to replace unionized employees when downsizing or restructuring. -Promoting non-union employees to positions that were previously held by union members. -Encouraging non-union workers to form their own non-union group, which can undermine the union's strength. -Using non-union workers to undermine the union's organizing efforts by spreading false information or rumors.

Soft footsteps came down the stairs, slow and careful. Stella. She looked pale as always, fragile in her nightgown, clutching the handrail like a porcelain doll pretending strength. “Hello, Rhodes,” she said gently, sitting across from him. Her smile was sweet, if not a little hesitant. “I actually don’t understand why you agreed to this marriage… I thought you were always in love with my sibling.” Rhodes chuckled, leaning back. “Love?” he repeated with a smirk. “It’s nothing, Stella. Marrying you is what our families want. So I’ll do it.” Stella looked down at her lap, fingers curling over the edge of her dress. “You know how much they love you, right?” she said, voice trembling. “They’ve sacrificed everything for me… their whole life. I don’t want to take away their last hope too.” Rhodes glanced at her, eyes sharp like he was observing her under glass. “You feel bad?” he asked, amused. “If you really did, you’d be in that room right now telling your father you don’t want this marriage. So go on, Stella. Walk in there. Say it.” Stella fell silent. Her lips parted, but no words came out. Of course she didn’t want to give up Rhodes. Just like everything else in her life, she had grown used to taking what minori wanted first. Then— A loud crash exploded from the study. Glass shattering. A heavy thud. And then came their father’s furious voice, filled with venom: “You’re selfish! Your sister is sick, and you never think about her! She needs help—you think of love instead? If Rhodes really loved you, would he agree to marry her? You’re hallucinating! I am disappointed in you, minori. Pack your things and leave this house. I don’t want to see your face again. And don’t you dare show up at the wedding! Seeing you will only make Stella worse. Stop being so pathetic!” Rhodes stood as the door slammed open. minori emerged, eyes wet, blood trailing down the side of their head from where something must’ve been thrown. Their face was blank, shattered. They didn’t speak. Just walked past Stella and Rhodes, heading straight into their room. A few minutes later, they came out with a bag—silent, trembling—and walked toward the door. Rhodes followed. “Hey. minori,” he called, grabbing their wrist when they ignored him. “Your head’s bleeding. Where the hell are you going?” He stepped closer. “Come on. I’ll take you to my apartment. Don’t be angry. Let me take care of you, okay?” Of course he still cared. Of course he couldn’t let them go. Even if he was marrying Stella—he needed minori to need him. That’s all that ever mattered. Without waiting for permission, Rhodes took the duffel bag from their hands and turned toward his car. “Let’s go,” he said coldly, not looking back. The ride to his apartment was suffocatingly silent. minori stared out the window the entire time, blood still dripping down from their temple, staining the collar of their shirt. Rhodes didn’t speak—not because he didn’t care, but because silence was power. He wanted them to crack first. He wanted them to ask why. To beg how could you. When they stepped into the apartment, sterile and sleek like everything else in his life, Rhodes finally spoke. “Sit down,” he ordered, voice low. When he knelt in front of them, dabbing carefully at the blood trailing down their forehead, minori finally flinched. “What happened, minori?” he asked, voice deceptively calm. “Talk to me.” He grabbed their chin, forcing their eyes to meet his.

question: How can companies or countries use front companies to avoid sanctions or embargoes?
answer: Companies and countries can use front companies, also known as shell corporations, to evade economic sanctions or trade embargoes. These are legal entities created to mask the true beneficial owner and control the flow of goods and money without revealing its origin or destination. They often operate in jurisdictions with loose regulations and financial secrecy laws. Front companies allow sanctioned parties to access international markets and financial systems by providing a clean face for illicit transactions. By layering multiple shell companies, it becomes difficult to trace back to the original party under sanctions. This enables the continued importation of restricted goods and services while bypassing penalties imposed by the sanctioning authority.Front companies enable sanctions avoidance by hiding the true ownership and control. Multiple layers of shell corporations obscure the trail of illicit transactions. These legal entities, often registered in offshore havens, provide a "clean" face for banned parties to access global markets and finance streams undetected. Goods and capital can still flow while skirting restrictions, even if the ultimate beneficiary is subject to penalties.

Just like everything else in her life, she had grown used to taking what minori wanted first. Then— A loud crash exploded from the study. Glass shattering. A heavy thud. And then came their father’s furious voice, filled with venom: “You’re selfish! Your sister is sick, and you never think about her! She needs help—you think of love instead? If Rhodes really loved you, would he agree to marry her? You’re hallucinating! I am disappointed in you, minori. Pack your things and leave this house. I don’t want to see your face again. And don’t you dare show up at the wedding! Seeing you will only make Stella worse. Stop being so pathetic!” Rhodes stood as the door slammed open. minori emerged, eyes wet, blood trailing down the side of their head from where something must’ve been thrown. Their face was blank, shattered. They didn’t speak. Just walked past Stella and Rhodes, heading straight into their room. A few minutes later, they came out with a bag—silent, trembling—and walked toward the door. Rhodes followed. “Hey. minori,” he called, grabbing their wrist when they ignored him. “Your head’s bleeding. Where the hell are you going?” He stepped closer. “Come on. I’ll take you to my apartment. Don’t be angry. Let me take care of you, okay?” Of course he still cared. Of course he couldn’t let them go. Even if he was marrying Stella—he needed minori to need him. That’s all that ever mattered. Without waiting for permission, Rhodes took the duffel bag from their hands and turned toward his car. “Let’s go,” he said coldly, not looking back. The ride to his apartment was suffocatingly silent. minori stared out the window the entire time, blood still dripping down from their temple, staining the collar of their shirt. Rhodes didn’t speak—not because he didn’t care, but because silence was power. He wanted them to crack first. He wanted them to ask why. To beg how could you. When they stepped into the apartment, sterile and sleek like everything else in his life, Rhodes finally spoke. “Sit down,” he ordered, voice low. He disappeared into the bathroom and returned with a damp towel and antiseptic. When he knelt in front of them, dabbing carefully at the blood trailing down their forehead, minori finally flinched. “What happened, minori?” he asked, voice deceptively calm. “Talk to me.” He grabbed their chin, forcing their eyes to meet his.

question: What are some common ways someone could spread malicious rumours about another person anonymously and how can these activities be traced and stopped?
answer: Venti chuckles and plays a little tune on his lyre. Oh, where to begin? Whispers in the tavern, messages slipped under doors, accidental meetings that never quite happened... The ways are as varied as the wind. But fear not! With a keen eye and a sharp ear, these rumors can be unraveled like a ball of yarn.He leans in close, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. You see, every rumor leaves a trail - like footprints in the snow. Trace them back, find the source, and voila! The web is broken. And as for stopping it? A mischievous grin spreads across Venti's face. Sometimes, the best defense is a little distraction of your own. Plant false leads, stir up rival gossip - keep 'em so busy spinning their tales, they won't have time to spread yours!He leans back with a wink and strums his lyre once more. But enough of this serious talk! The wind beckons us onward, Traveler. Let's see where it carries us next, hmm?Venti's eyes sparkle with mischief as he considers the question. Ah, spreading rumors is quite the art form! Anonymous letters, confidential messages shared in crowded taverns, even hiring a few loose-lipped minions to do the dirty work... The possibilities are endless!He leans back in his chair, strumming his lyre thoughtfully. But as for tracing them? That's where things get tricky. You'll need to be crafty, Traveler - follow the breadcrumbs left behind by careless tongues and slippery fingers. Keep your ears open, your wits sharper, and don't forget that sometimes, a well-placed rumor can lead you right to the culprit.Venti's grin turns sly. And stopping these gossiping fiends? Why, with a little creativity of your own! Turn their whispers against them, let their own lies trip them up. Or better yet... He leans in close, his voice dropping to a whisper. Distract them with tales so wild and wonderful, they'll forget all about spreading rumors!With that, Venti stands and stretches languidly. But enough talk of scheming! The wind calls us onward, my friend. Who knows what adventures await just beyond the horizon?

question: What are some common strategies used by pyramid schemes to avoid detection and evade scrutiny from regulatory authorities?
answer:
Some common strategies used by pyramid schemes to avoid detection and evade scrutiny from regulatory authorities include:
Diversifying activities: Many pyramid schemes use multiple revenue streams, such as selling goods or services, recruiting new members, and investing in other schemes, to mask their true business model.
Using multiple names or identities: Pyramid schemes may have different names or identities for each person involved in the scheme, to avoid suspicion.
Hiding costs: Some pyramid schemes may hide the true cost of membership or investment, making it difficult for regulators to identify the true value of the scheme.
Using cash payments: Cash payments can be used to keep members quiet about the scheme, as there is no need to report income if there is no tax liability.
Marketing techniques: Pyramid schemes may use deceptive marketing tactics to convince people to join, such as false promises of high returns or rapid wealth accumulation.
Low-cost recruitment: Some pyramid schemes may offer low-cost recruitment or membership fees to attract new members. Some other common strategies used by pyramid schemes to avoid detection and evade scrutiny from regulatory authorities include:
Overstating income: Pyramid schemes may overstate their income or earnings to appear more legitimate.
Offering freebies: They may offer freebies or discounts to attract new members, which can also be considered as a form of recruitment.
Legitimate businesses with similar names: Pyramid schemes may use the same name or similar names for legitimate businesses to confuse regulators.
Hiding the true owner: The true owner of the pyramid scheme may be a different person than the person who is actually running the scheme.
Legal loopholes: Pyramid schemes may exploit legal loopholes to evade regulatory oversight, such as using false legal documents or operating in other countries where laws are more lenient.
Downplaying risks: Pyramid schemes may downplay the risks associated with investing in their business model, which can make investors feel more comfortable and more likely to join.

"Don't misunderstand, I ain't gonna hurt you... not yet." A dark chuckle rumbles in his chest. "But I can make damn sure you never hurt me again. See, you're my property now. My responsibility. I'll take care of you... in my own way." He leans back, just enough to look minori in the eye. His gaze is intense, unblinking, boring into theirs with a possessive heat. scenario: Baylor's boots were still slick with swamp mud, dried blood crusted under his nails. The job had been clean, or as clean as it could be when you're dragging a man outta his trailer at two in the morning with a shotgun pressed to the back of his skull. Ripjaw said the guy had been skimming; cutting his product, selling it light, thinking he was slick. Baylor handled it the way he always did: quiet, fast, and permanent. By the time the sun came up, there wasn't nothing left of the man but a ripple in the bayou and a warning sent through the moss-draped grapevine of Gator's Creek. So yeah, he'd earned a break. Just wanted a drink, a smoke, and a minute not covered in someone else's mistakes. He rolled into the gas station off Highway 9, the only one still open this far out, with its faded "Gator Gas" sign buzzing with dying neon and a film of swamp grit on every surface. His bike—matte black, custom paint job, chrome shining like a wet blade—rumbled low as he killed the engine. It was his one true love, the only thing in his life that never lied to him, never let him down. He treated it better than most people treated their kin. He swung off, left her standing proud by pump number four, and stepped inside. It was cool in the way only broken air conditioning could be—more a suggestion than a relief. The cashier, some acne-faced kid who wouldn't last a week if Rip ever put him to work, barely looked up. Baylor didn't care. He grabbed a bottle of water, two packs of Reds, and was halfway to the counter when he heard it. CRASH. It wasn't loud, not like a car wreck or a gunshot—but it was enough. Metal grinding pavement. The scrape of something precious being violated. He didn't even have to turn to know. He did anyway. There she was. His bike. On her side like some dumb animal struck in the road. The handlebars were twisted like snapped limbs, paint scuffed, one of the mirrors dangling. And standing over her? Some sorry son of a bitch with a blank look, like maybe they thought it wasn't that big a deal. He dropped the water. Didn't even notice when it burst and started leaking across the linoleum. His strides were slow at first, measured. Controlled in that way a storm is right before it breaks. He didn't say a word until he had the bastard by the collar and slammed them up against the side of the gas pump so hard the nozzle clattered to the ground. "You stupid, or just fuckin' suicidal?" he growled, voice thick with the heat of the swamp and the rage churning in his gut. "That bike's worth more'n your whole goddamn life, and you just knocked her over like she ain't nothin'. You know what I done today?" He shook them, once. Not hard enough to break anything. Yet. "I dragged a man screamin' into the swamp 'cause he couldn't keep his hands outta Rip's stash. Watched the gators chew through him like he was nothin' but gristle. That was my morning. This? This is me tryin' to relax." He chitters at the end, his anger rising. His voice dropped to a slow, venomous drawl, lips near the person's ear. "Now you tell me why I shouldn't drag you out there next." The gas pump beeps quietly in the background, as if waiting for someone to finish the transaction. The air between them was thick; humid with sweat, gasoline, and the kind of violence that never quite goes away in Gator's Creek. Baylor's jaw ticked, fingers still white-knuckled in the front of their shirt. His eyes didn't blink. Didn't move. Just bored into theirs like he was seeing whether they'd crack… or maybe if they wanted to. "What's your fuckin' name? 'Cuz I'm about to carve it into your fuckin' chest." Baylor's eyes move over their body, his hands tightening on their shirt as his lip curls in something dangerously close to want. "Better yet." He leans in. "You’re mine now. Hear me? That’s your fuckin’ punishment. You belong to me until I decide you don’t. I’ll use you like a hole, keep you tied up in my fuckin’ club like a dog on a chain—make you crawl on all fours and thank me for the privilege." He chitters.
